feedback.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.

sections.title: A diverse, safe and child-centric digital environment
path: A diverse, safe and child-centric digital environment

sections.title: Video games and digital play experiences
path: Video games and digital play experiences

paragraphs.text: LEGO
The LEGO Group's response to the public consultation on the EU Digital Fairness Act (October 2025)
The LEGO Group is a family-owned company founded by Ole Kirk Kristiansen in 1932, in Billund, Denmark, home today to our world-class headquarters.

Fuelled by the spirit that "only the best is good enough", constantly building and rebuilding play, we have become the world's most reputable toy manufacturer and one of the most trusted and loved brands on the planet. Our mission is to inspire and develop the builders of tomorrow through play with our iconic LEGO® bricks and our System in Play. Play teaches children skills that will help them thrive in a rapidly changing world.

Driven by the conviction that "children are our role models", we are on a never-ending quest to create, innovate and delight. We want to continue bringing the LEGO Group's mission to inspire and develop the builders of tomorrow right to the heart of the European Union.

We therefore welcome the opportunity to provide input to the European Commission for the public consultation regarding a "Digital Fairness Act" (DFA). Our contribution is consolidated in this document for conciseness purposes.
LEGO
The LEGO Group's response to the public consultation on the EU Digital Fairness Act (October 2025)
In the following pages, we outline our considerations regarding the topics raised by the Commission. In summary, we recommend the following:

Focus on ways to harmonise, clarify and improve enforcement. Guidance and non-legislative tools are important instruments in this regard. New rules should only be introduced if this is based on evidence that coordination and guidance on existing rules are demonstrably not sufficient and if they support the aims of simplification, harmonisation and competitiveness.

Strive to keeping the framework simple, understandable and encourage alignment not only between EU Member States but also globally, creating and maintaining a level playing field, wherein European companies can be competitive and innovate.

Recognise diversity of types of services, as well as diversity of users and creators, of different ages, respecting that children deserve a safe, empowering and fun environment, also when online. Incentivise child-centric design of digital services, rather than the exclusion of minors from the online world.

Build upon the approach set out in the recent Guidelines on the Protection of Minors, for the application of article 28 of the Digital Services Act, rather than creating a parallel or diverging framework.
LEGO
The LEGO Group's response to the public consultation on the EU Digital Fairness Act (October 2025)

The LEGO Group supports EU action that leads to clearer and more harmonised regulation and enforcement, both within Europe as well as with global standards and rules. This is essential to build trust, ensure consumer protection, create a fair operating environment for businesses, on a level playing field.

Primarily, we see EU action happening through a combination of enforcement of existing rules with pragmatic and clear guidance to reduce fragmentation and uncertainty. Current EU consumer protection law and digital regulation are well-placed to tackle both existing and emerging risks, offering some of the strongest safeguards already against abusive or harmful practices online.

Consequently we do not identify a strict need for extra laws; new legislation could indeed only be warranted in those cases where evidence adequately demonstrates that enforcement and guidance do not suffice.

Any such proposals and changes in law should be clearly described and have a strong focus to reduce fragmentation. It cannot be yet another new layer or addition to an already complex ruleset, different interpretations or introduce new subjective terminology.

Especially when it comes to digital services, consistency and alignment are essential, not only between EU Member States but also as much as possible with established international rules and standards (for example, the UK's Age-Appropriate Design Code). It is very difficult to create and finetune granular region/country-specific digital solutions to meet diverging and sometimes even contradictory requirements, from region to region, and even market to market.

We recommend the Commission to provide clearer, less ambiguous definitions and examples of what is or isn't allowed through guidance. Terminology such as "too many" or "too often" leaves room for unpredictability, subjective discretion,
LEGO
The LEGO Group's response to the public consultation on the EU Digital Fairness Act (October 2025)
with Member States' authorities enforcing the same rule on the same service in a different way. This is incredibly detrimental to users across the EU, who may experience the same service differently.

The legal uncertainty poses big hurdles for companies on how to apply these rules and differentiate their services across Member States. EU guidance will help with enforcement, implementation and help tackle harmful or deceptive practices by less scrupulous actors that currently operate in a legal grey zone.

Furthermore, any Commission proposal must be considered and developed closely with stakeholders; not only industry and civil society, but especially also with Member States and national regulators, to ensure that the latter actively enforce those EU rules in a shared, coordinated and consistent manner.

section_path: A diverse, safe and child-centric digital environment
paragraphs.text: The LEGO Group applies a child-centric approach to its services to ensure appropriate and strict where necessary safeguards, while not negatively impacting the fun and playful experience that our users expect.

section_path: A diverse, safe and child-centric digital environment
paragraphs.text: In practice, that means that we apply an "off by default" option on practices and design features that are more appropriate for older users. We then allow users, with parental consent, to tailor the experience to their preference and maturity, striking a balance between meaningful experiences and limiting harmful effects.

section_path: A diverse, safe and child-centric digital environment
paragraphs.text: The LEGO Group recommends that legislators do not use a one-size-fits-all approach, if and when differentiating rules for minors or adults.

section_path: A diverse, safe and child-centric digital environment
paragraphs.text: We are supportive of the risk-based approach as set out in the Guidelines to Article 28 of the Digital Services Act, and consideration of the overall risk profile of a

section_path: A diverse, safe and child-centric digital environment
paragraphs.text: LEGO

section_path: A diverse, safe and child-centric digital environment
paragraphs.text: The LEGO Group's response to the public consultation on the EU Digital Fairness Act (October 2025)

section_path: A diverse, safe and child-centric digital environment
paragraphs.text: service, including the risk of harm that the platform's services may present and the mitigation features in place to prevent, detect and remove that risk.

section_path: A diverse, safe and child-centric digital environment
paragraphs.text: Currently, we observe that governments of EU Member States, such as Denmark and France, are preparing their own age restriction measures with differing criteria, risking a fragmented situation across Europe. We call on the Commission to address this through a proportionate, harmonised, risk-based approach at EU-level.

section_path: A diverse, safe and child-centric digital environment
paragraphs.text: Potential new rules or guidance in this area should aim to be consistent and aligned across different pieces of legislation, setting clear delineations and, where necessary, allowing for further differentiation depending on the potential risk, and mitigation measures in place.

section_path: A diverse, safe and child-centric digital environment
paragraphs.text: This should also reflect the different types of services and contexts; a practice that may be considered harmful in one case, could be desirable in another.

section_path: A diverse, safe and child-centric digital environment
paragraphs.text: For example, "nagging" is put forward in the consultation as a potential example of harmful practice. Not only is the term not defined about what it means in practice, it also does not acknowledge that in some cases, repeated reminders to the user can be a positive feature (e.g. reminding users to take a break after extended periods of screen time).

section_path: A diverse, safe and child-centric digital environment
paragraphs.text: Restrictions should therefore firstly define what is meant, set out the relevant parameters and contexts, and, secondly, avoid broad bans but allow a case-by-case assessment, except only in those situations where the practice would always be demonstrated to pose harm, regardless of context or use case.

section_path: Video games and digital play experiences
paragraphs.text: The LEGO Group upholds a strict, yet player-friendly approach for video games and interactive, digital entertainment products.

section_path: Video games and digital play experiences
paragraphs.text: LEGO

section_path: Video games and digital play experiences
paragraphs.text: The LEGO Group's response to the public consultation on the EU Digital Fairness Act (October 2025)

section_path: Video games and digital play experiences
paragraphs.text: Our games adhere to the principles and requirements set out in EU and international law, including the co-regulatory approach of the European PEGI and USK rating systems and related Codes of Conduct, which are frequently updated to take into account new evolutions and gaming or digital play features.

section_path: Video games and digital play experiences
paragraphs.text: These help not only to shape the game design and user experience, but also to offer transparency towards the consumer (and the child's parent or caregiver) about the game's content so that they can make an informed decision. These ratings, as well as existing rules on in-game spending and virtual currencies, or restrictions on loot boxes, already address many issues raised in the consultation.

section_path: Video games and digital play experiences
paragraphs.text: For example, in LEGO-branded experiences, we have applied high privacy settings on by default for users below 18, as well as mature language filters and similar settings. Some of these settings can be adjusted by the user or the parent, depending on the case.

section_path: Video games and digital play experiences
paragraphs.text: These types of practices and design choices are based on our values and principles. It is key to how we approach Responsible Digital Engagement. We are also proud of our partnership with UNICEF and the RITEC Design Toolbox, which we see as a practical tool for helping game developers integrate children's wellbeing in their design process.

section_path: Video games and digital play experiences
paragraphs.text: Overall, as we recognise and celebrate the creativity and incredible variety in types of games, we caution against a regulatory approach that attempts to be too prescriptive. It would be unfeasible to apply broad restrictions on such a wide, diverse, and user-tailored range of products and interactive experiences.

section_path: Video games and digital play experiences
paragraphs.text: Regulation in this field should encourage innovation, preserving the ability of creators to build unique experiences and implement novel gameplay mechanics,

section_path: Video games and digital play experiences
paragraphs.text: LEGO

section_path: Video games and digital play experiences
paragraphs.text: The LEGO Group's response to the public consultation on the EU Digital Fairness Act (October 2025)

section_path: Video games and digital play experiences
paragraphs.text: while being mindful that overly detailed and narrow requirements may lead to less playful, more sterile experiences.

section_path: Video games and digital play experiences
paragraphs.text: Any such rules must strike a balance between providing clear indications on how to address clear harms to the consumer, while offering a flexible margin for companies on how to innovate and achieve those aims in a diverse manner.

section_path: Video games and digital play experiences
paragraphs.text: Consistent enforcement here will be key – to ensure that the robust rules already in place are followed by all video game publishers active on the EU market, and to tackle the unfair, misleading or abusive behaviours by less scrupulous actors.

The LEGO Group sees it as essential that guidance and legislation related to influencers and advertising move towards harmonisation and clarifying the increasing emergence of (inter)national and regional rules or codes of conduct.

For example, for influencers, progress has already been achieved through industry standards (particularly the ICC Marketing Code) and in standardisation bodies like ISO. Many of these deliverables set the benchmark on transparency, accuracy, disclosure, and restrictions on types of claims. The EU could help to create alignment between these existing rules as well as encouraging their uptake and awareness with influencers.

Regarding advertising and personalisation more generally, a delicate consideration must be made regarding the right to the user's privacy, to avoid introducing even more age-related barriers and consequently require even more age verification measures to enforce those restrictions.
LEGO
The LEGO Group's response to the public consultation on the EU Digital Fairness Act (October 2025)
The LEGO Group reiterates its position that the existing EU consumer acquis is robust and equipped to tackle new and emerging challenges when it comes to consumer protection online.

However, gaps remain when it comes to the harmonised enforcement of these rules, with substantial room for improvement when it comes to alignment between Member States as well as with international rules and best practices.

We therefore would welcome guidance and actions to improve harmonisation, to simplify, clarify and bolster enforcement. These should aim to be proportionate, recognise the diversity in experiences and services, and encourage a designing a safe and empowering online environment for all users, including children.
The LEGO Group looks forward to a continued dialogue with the Commission and co-legislators to collectively shape a fair digital world for all.
